--- SOURCE 4: Modeling and Prediction of Land Use Land Cover Change ... - MDPI ---
URL: https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/13/7/3740

SUMMARY:
The aim of the study was to assess the temporal and spatial LULC dynamics of the past and to predict the future using Landsat images and LCM (Land Change Modeler) by considering the drivers of LULC dynamics. Converting the forest area, range land, and grass land into other land uses, especially to agricultural land, is the main LULC change in the future. The analysis of LULC change in Nashe watershed, its drivers, and prediction of future land use change are vital for understanding the dynamics of human environment interactions and environmental management interventions. Therefore, it is very important to assess and predict the LULC changes based on the historical data using Landsat images and Land Change Modeler.

# Modeling and Prediction of Land Use Land Cover Change Dynamics Based on Land Change Modeler (LCM) in Nashe Watershed, Upper Blue Nile Basin, Ethiopia

by

Megersa Kebede Leta

## Abstract

Change of land use land cover (LULC) has been known globally as an essential driver of environmental change. Assessment of LULC change is the most precise method to comprehend the past land use, types of changes to be estimated, the forces and developments behind the changes. The aim of the study was to assess the temporal and spatial LULC dynamics of the past and to predict the future using Landsat images and LCM (Land Change Modeler) by considering the drivers of LULC dynamics. The research was conducted in Nashe watershed (Ethiopia) which is the main tributary of the Upper Blue Nile basin. The total watershed area is 94,578 ha. The Landsat imagery from 2019, 2005, and 1990 was used for evaluating and predicting the spatiotemporal distributions of LULC changes. The future LULC image prediction has been generated depending on the historical trends of LULC changes for the years 2035 and 2050. LCM integrated in TerrSet Geospatial Monitoring and Modeling System assimilated with MLP and CA-Markov chain have been used for monitoring, assessment of change, and future projections. Markov chain was used to generate transition probability matrices between LULC classes and cellular automata were used to predict the LULC map. Validation of the predicted LULC map of 2019 was conducted successfully with the actual LULC map. The validation accuracy was determined using the Kappa statistics and agreement/disagreement marks. The results of the historical LULC depicted that forest land, grass land, and range land are the most affected types of land use. The agricultural land in 1990 was 41,587.21 ha which increased to 57,868.95 ha in 2019 with an average growth rate of 39.15%. The forest land, range land, and grass land declined annually with rates of 48.38%, 19.58%, and 26.23%, respectively. The predicted LULC map shows that the forest cover will further degrade from 16.94% in 2019 to 8.07% in 2050, while agricultural land would be expanded to 69,021.20 ha and 69,264.44 ha in 2035 and 2050 from 57,868.95 ha in 2019. The findings of this investigation indicate an expected rapid change in LULC for the coming years. Converting the forest area, range land, and grass land into other land uses, especially to agricultural land, is the main LULC change in the future. Measures should be implemented to achieve rational use of agricultural land and the forest conversion needs to be well managed.

Keywords:

[land change modeler](/search?q=land+change+modeler); [Landsat images](/search?q=Landsat+images); [modeling LULC change](/search?q=modeling+LULC+change); [multilayer perceptron](/search?q=multilayer+perceptron); [TerrSet](/search?q=TerrSet)

## 1. Introduction

Land use land cover (LULC) change occurs under a variety of pressure and it is the result of changes or modifications in the intensity of an existing LULC type to determine the location and nature of the land use change. The changes of LULC have been perceived as worldwide environmental change drivers in the watershed that are very sensitive to LULC dynamics [[1](#B1-sustainability-13-03740)]. A dynamic LULC offers an inclusive sympathetic of the interactions and relations that are crucial for sustainable land resource management [[2](#B2-sustainability-13-03740)]. At global and local levels, the changes of LULC are driven by anthropogenic and natural processes at different spatiotemporal levels. The LULC changes are dynamic, non-linear human–nature interactions that are significant land surface conversions and involve complex processes. The LULC change trajectory worldwide for the past 300 years has been categorized by gains in agriculture and losses in forests [[3](#B3-sustainability-13-03740),[4](#B4-sustainability-13-03740)]. According to the authors of [[5](#B5-sustainability-13-03740)], LULC changes are associated with the change of forest land to agricultural expansion, urbanization, and deforestation.

The LULC changes were broadly assessed in various areas of the world, for instance, Europe and USA [[6](#B6-sustainability-13-03740)], South America [[7](#B7-sustainability-13-03740)], Asia, and Africa [[8](#B8-sustainability-13-03740)], Ethiopia [[9](#B9-sustainability-13-03740),[10](#B10-sustainability-13-03740)]. In Africa, the expansion of agricultural land influenced by rapid population growth has been recognized as a primary driver of LUC. Many developed countries including Europe and the United states practiced massive deforestation because of agricultural expansion and industrialization until the early 19th century [[11](#B11-sustainability-13-03740)]. Urbanization dynamics and urban growth are usually linked to demographic factors mostly in developing countries [[12](#B12-sustainability-13-03740)]. In Africa, the expansion of agriculture influenced by population growth has been recognized as a primary driver of land use land cover change (LULCC). The landscape has been intensely changed due to socio-economic and political changes that occurred in the first half of the 19th century in Europe [[11](#B11-sustainability-13-03740)]. The decrease in agricultural land at the expenditure of increased urban areas and water bodies in Europe is another strong trend [[13](#B13-sustainability-13-03740)].

The dynamics of LULC intensities and rates are changing because they are highly associated with the overexploitation of natural resources. The natural variability issues like climate change, soil conditions, and terrain characteristics have also accounted for land use changes [[14](#B14-sustainability-13-03740)]. Therefore, the integration of natural and human factors in LULC dynamics have become a significant issue throughout the world in efficient land use. The assessment of LULC change and the drivers that have direct consequences on the natural environment and human societies are the focus of the current scientific examination of scientists [[15](#B15-sustainability-13-03740)]. In order to develop sustainable strategies and to have informed planning decisions, understanding the drivers and dynamics of LULC change is crucial. The LULC change driving forces can be direct or indirect for change over time and space; this was incorporated to provide an estimate of future scenarios [[16](#B16-sustainability-13-03740)].

The drivers’ assessment and predicting their future LULC status in the watershed is expected to have an essential contribution for land use planning management and sustainable water resources. The use of historical satellite imageries are used to effectively monitor and analyze LULC change [[17](#B17-sustainability-13-03740)]. Future LULC change analysis and prediction are often complicated for the stochastic change of nature and the dynamic of natural and socioeconomic variables. The prediction of forthcoming LULC dynamics in areas where the economic condition depends upon agriculture has a profound impact. The LULC model developed in this study predicts a future LULC state based on a business-as-usual scenario. Therefore, understanding the earlier and current LULC changes and simulating for the future is vital for the management of land and water resources of the basin [[18](#B18-sustainability-13-03740),[19](#B19-sustainability-13-03740)].

Models of land change are useful tools for environmental and other types of research concerning LULC change [[20](#B20-sustainability-13-03740)]. The magnitude and location are the two important issues of LULC that have been considered in the modeling. LULC change assessment models are either dynamic or static, non-spatial or spatial, deductive or inductive, pattern-based or agent-based [[21](#B21-sustainability-13-03740),[22](#B22-sustainability-13-03740)]. Modeling the LULC process is to properly calibrate and validate the model for predicting future changes [[23](#B23-sustainability-13-03740)]. The LCM embedded in the TerrSet model has been utilized to inspect the historical and to predict the future changes of LULC of the watershed. LCM primarily uses the MLPNN-CA-MC approach. The model is strong due to its dynamic projection proficiency, suitable calibration, and capability to simulate several types of land cover [[24](#B24-sustainability-13-03740),[25](#B25-sustainability-13-03740)]. LCM evaluates changes of the land use of two different periods, determines the changes, visualizes changes, and presents the results with various maps and graphs.

The model predicts future LULC images based upon MLPNN (Multi-Layer Perceptron neural networks) and CA-Markov Chain (CA-MC) [[26](#B26-sustainability-13-03740)]. The “transition potential” of pixel to change into another class determination is through MLP drivers of change [[27](#B27-sustainability-13-03740)]. The MC model is stochastic, which requires pairs of LULC images to derive the transition potential into the future predictions based on the amount of historical change [[28](#B28-sustainability-13-03740),[29](#B29-sustainability-13-03740)]. The blend of CA-MC can simulate the spatiotemporal dynamics of LULC change. The model predicts transitions of a cell from one LULC to another depending on “physical and socio-economic” data [[30](#B30-sustainability-13-03740),[31](#B31-sustainability-13-03740)]. The model validation process is the accuracy assessment of prediction and contrast made between the predicted and observed land cover maps. Therefore, to assess the change drivers of the past and to simulate for the future, the validated data have been used.

Remote sensing data particularly Landsat images provide suitable possibility for LULC change monitoring, particularly for developing countries where geospatial technologies are not well developed [[32](#B32-sustainability-13-03740)]. In Africa, around 40% of the population were living in urban centers as of 2014. By 2050 the urban residents’ percentage has been projected to reach 56% [[33](#B33-sustainability-13-03740)]. In Europe, between 1990 and 2006 the population grew up by 146% [[34](#B34-sustainability-13-03740)] as per the study was conducted in 24 European countries. Currently in Europe around 73% of the population lives in urban areas and it is estimated to be over 80% by 2050 [[33](#B33-sustainability-13-03740)]. The availability of resources as well as their dynamics and management varies considerably from area to area, especially in Ethiopia. Ethiopia is highly vulnerable to environmental changes induced by natural and anthropogenic activities. LULC change is a fundamental problem in the country [[35](#B35-sustainability-13-03740)].

Due to changes of LULC, Ethiopia experienced serious environmental problems including soil erosion, land degradation, loss of soil fertility, and deforestation [[36](#B36-sustainability-13-03740)]. The “Upper Blue Nile Basin (UBNB)” is the most varied and a highly important river basin in Ethiopia [[37](#B37-sustainability-13-03740),[38](#B38-sustainability-13-03740)]. Nashe watershed is the tributary of the Basin that faced LULC change driven by population growth, urbanization, agricultural land expansion, deforestation, overgrazing, expansion of industrial activities, and political dynamics. The analysis of LULC change in Nashe watershed, its drivers, and prediction of future land use change are vital for understanding the dynamics of human environment interactions and environmental management interventions. Therefore, it is very important to assess and predict the LULC changes based on the historical data using Landsat images and Land Change Modeler. The following research questions were addressed: (i) what was the trend of LULC change within the study area in the past (1990–2019) and which LULC classes were mostly affected? (ii) What growth and change patterns can be expected in the future? (iii) What are the major driving factors of LULC change in the watershed?

The above research questions are so significant and addressed in this study of watershed since a dramatic socio-economic change with expected enormous effect on the land use is undergoing and this will influence various hydrological processes. Thus, especially in the UBNB, those changes need to be predicted in time for environmental management in this area in an effective and sustainable manner. The study findings are utilized to provide empirical evidence on patterns and rates and identify major driving forces of LULC dynamics at watershed level, and improve policies in land use within the framework of sustainable land use planning in relation to future changes or development.

## 2. Materials and Methods

#### 2.1. Study Area

The Upper Blue Nile Basin (UBNB) is the main stream of the Nile basin and is located within the western and central part of Ethiopia between latitudes 7°45′ and 12°45′ N and longitudes 34°05′ to 39°45′ E. The UBNB consists of the major part of Ethiopia and covers an area of 157,000 km2. The basin is located within the region of Oromia, Amhara and Benishangul-Gumuz of Ethiopia. The basin has three main seasons: a main rainy season which occurs between June and September, a dry season from October to January, and a short rainy season between February and May. The basin mean yearly rainfall ranges within 800–2000 mm and increases with altitude. The Nashe watershed is the major tributary from the left bank within the UBNB of Ethiopia which is situated at about 300 km from Addis Ababa. The sub-basin lies in between 9°35′ and 9°52′ N latitudes and 37°00′ and 37°20′ E longitudes ([Figure 1](#fig_body_display_sustainability-13-03740-f001)).

**Figure 1.**
Map of the study area.

The watershed area varies in elevation from 1600 m in the lower plateau under the escarpment to the hills and ridges of the highland climbing to over 2500 m. The annual average rainfall of the Nashe watershed ranges from 1200 mm to 1600 mm (depending on data from five weather stations); June, July, August, and September are the main rainy season of the catchment. The observed average temperature of the catchment is 22 °C. The watershed area is categorized by intensive irrigable lands at the downstream, large water potential sites at the upstream, and also with high head of hydropower potential. Agriculture is the leading financial activity in the watershed and the main source of livelihood for the local population.

#### 2.2. Data Types and Sources

The important spatial data required for the study were Digital Elevation Model, Landsat Images, and field data. The three Landsat images used were downloaded from the USGS (<http://glovis.usgs.gov/>, accessed on 2 January 2017) ([Table 1](#table_body_display_sustainability-13-03740-t001)) and DEM was obtained from the Ethiopian Ministry of Water, Irrigation and Energy (MoWIE). The collected 30 m spatial resolution of DEM was used to delineate the watershed, to develop elevation, and to generate the slope of the watershed. The analysis was performed using the TM Landsat-5 from 1990, ETM+ Landsat-7 from 2005, and OLI\_TIRS Landsat-8 from 2019. The images were acquired in January, which corresponds to the dry season in Ethiopia when a clear sky period occurs to obtain images with Zero clouds and avoid extreme differences in the land cover reflectance dataset. The global positioning system (GPS) measurements were taken during fieldwork to verify and confirm the information gathered through remote sensing at each ground control points (GCP). The LULC types were noted and for reference purposes, photographs were also taken.

**Table 1.**
Details of Landsat images data used for the analysis of land use and land cover (LULC) in the study area.

The coordinates of each location selected were marked with GPS, and these points were verified in Google Earth. During field survey and data collection, socioeconomic survey methods such as interviews with key informants, discussions with focus groups, and participatory field observation were conducted. The socio-economic data collected were utilized to get information on historical land use change, socio-economic status, and the driving factors that change the land use in the watershed. The key informants were chosen purposely from various social groups including elders, community leaders, and local natural resource experts.

#### 2.3. Land Use and Land Cover Change Assessment

#### 2.3.1. Image Classification

The classification of images is to categorize automatically all pixels from the Landsat images into LULC classes to extract useful thematic information [[39](#B39-sustainability-13-03740)]. For image classification, the data of ground truth were gathered on the field and satellite image verification. After pre-processing the satellite images, the supervised classification was implemented using “maximum likelihood classification (MLC)” technique to produce image classification. The MLC method is the widely used algorithm for supervised satellite image classification [[40](#B40-sustainability-13-03740)]. The method has a strong theoretical foundation and the ability to accommodate varying data, LULC types, and satellite systems [[28](#B28-sustainability-13-03740)]. The approach of supervised classification is adopted as it preserves the basic land cover characteristics through statistical classification techniques [[41](#B41-sustainability-13-03740)]. In classifying the 1990, 2005, and 2019 images, reference data were gathered from images of Google Earth and through interviews from concerning body of the corresponding time periods. In this study, six separable LULC are considered ([Table 2](#table_body_display_sustainability-13-03740-t002)).

**Table 2.**
Major land use land cover types used and their descriptions.

To accomplish classification of image in multi-temporal approach and for mapping purposes, the ERDAS Imagine 2015 and ArcGIS 10.3 software were used, respectively. For each LULC, as many as possible training samples were selected throughout the entire image, based on the composite images, as well as Google Earth images. For classification, verification, and validation of the classified images, the training data were used.

#### 2.3.2. Accuracy Assessment

Accuracy assessment tells us to what extent the ground truth is represented on the equivalent classified image. Since land use maps derived from image classification usually contain some errors, the accuracy of classification results obtained must be assessed. Assessing the classification accuracy provides the degree of confidence in the results and the subsequent change detection [[40](#B40-sustainability-13-03740)]. For accuracy assessment, the classified map was compared with ground truth data. For 1990 and 2005, the reference points were collected from Google Earth, original Landsat images, interviews, group discussion, previous reports, and maps. For the 2019 image, Google Earth, field observation, original Landsat images, interviews, and group discussions of random reference points in different LULC types were recorded from the field survey conducted by using GPS.

The common and most effective method used to measure the accuracy of the classified image from remotely sensed imagery is an error/confusion matrix [[42](#B42-sustainability-13-03740)]. The confusion matrix provides overall accuracy, user accuracy, producer accuracy, and kappa statistics. Kappa coefficient was determined by using Equation (1) [[43](#B43-sustainability-13-03740)]. According to the authors of [[21](#B21-sustainability-13-03740)], a kappa coefficient value below 0.4 shows poor agreement, a value between 0.4 and 0.8 depicts a moderate agreement, and a value greater than 0.8 shows a strong agreement.

𝐾=𝑁∑𝑟𝑖=1𝑋𝑖𝑖−∑𝑟𝑖=1(𝑋𝑖+)∗(𝑋+𝑖)𝑁2−∑𝑟𝑖=1(𝑋𝑖+)∗(𝑋+𝑖)

𝐾=(𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙∗𝑠𝑢𝑚 𝑜𝑓 𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑒𝑐𝑡)−𝑠𝑢𝑚 𝑜𝑓 𝑎𝑙𝑙 𝑡ℎ𝑒 (𝑟𝑜𝑤 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙∗𝑐𝑜𝑙𝑢𝑚𝑛 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙)𝑇𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑠𝑞𝑢𝑎𝑟𝑒𝑑−𝑠𝑢𝑚 𝑜𝑓 𝑎𝑙𝑙 𝑡ℎ𝑒 (𝑟𝑜𝑤 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙∗𝑐𝑜𝑙𝑢𝑚𝑛 𝑡𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙)

where r—rows number in the matrix, Xii—number of observations in row i and column i (the diagonal elements), X + i and Xi+—the marginal totals of row i and column I, respectively, and N—observations number.

#### 2.3.3. Land Use Land Cover Change Drivers

Driving factors influence LULC changes. LULC changes are driven by natural and human activities [[10](#B10-sustainability-13-03740)]. The regionally differing main drivers have great impact on many environmental aspects [[44](#B44-sustainability-13-03740)]. Provided that drivers of past changes are sustained, they can be expected to be influential forces in the future. LULC change simulation studies have used topographic and distance driver variables [[45](#B45-sustainability-13-03740),[46](#B46-sustainability-13-03740)]. Elevation, slope, distance from roads, distance from streams, distance from urban area, and evidence likelihood rasters were considered as the potential driver variables. Distance from roads, distance from streams, and distance from urban area were set as dynamic variables to express the varying distance as they change over time.

The evidence likelihood is an empirical probability of change in the LULC categories between an earlier and a later map [[47](#B47-sustainability-13-03740)]. It is used to transform categorical variables, such as change from one land cover class to another into numerical values. The significance of driver variables was tested using Cramer’s V and P values which measure strength of the correlation between two variable classes. Cramer’s V value is a coarse statistic measure of the association strength or dependency between variables, and it ranges from 0.0 to 1.0 in value. Generally, variables with a total Cramer’s V value greater than 0.15 are considered useful and those with a score over 0.4 are considered go

# Modeling and predicting land use and land cover changes using remote sensing in tropical coastal ecosystems of southern Peru

* [Ellen Delgado](#auth-Ellen-Delgado-Aff1-Aff2-Aff3)[1](#Aff1),[2](#Aff2),[3](#Aff3),
* [Elgar Barboza](#auth-Elgar-Barboza-Aff4)[4](#Aff4),

**volume 37**, Article number: 139 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

## Abstract

Understanding the spatial impacts of human activities on coastal marine ecosystems is fundamental to manage the dynamic changes in land use that affect these natural spaces. In this study, we assessed land-use and land-cover (LULC) changes from 1990 to 2020 and their projection to 2030 in the Ica region (Peru). Through the integration of geographic information systems (GIS) and remote sensing techniques, LULC changes were analyzed. The kappa index reported an accuracy of the LULC maps above 87% in the analysis period. In addition, the quantitative analysis revealed that in 1990, 2000, 2010 and 2020, cultivated areas increased by 48.9, 53.2, 60.11 and 75.72% in influence zones A1, A2, A3 and A4, respectively, while urban development increased by 2.84, 4.81, 4.82 and 7.82% ha in the same zones. Likewise, the loss and gain analysis of land cover by period revealed that, in 1990, 2000, 2010 and 2020, cultivated areas increased by 48.9, 53.2, 60.11 and 75.72% in the zones of influence A1, A2, A3 and A4, respectively, while urban development increased by 2.84, 4.81, 4.82 and 7.82% ha in the same zones. In addition, during the period 2010–2020, the rate of transformation reached 53.1 ha/year towards urban uses in the coastal zone (A3) and 981.2 ha/year towards crops in zone A4. By 2030, urban expansion along the coast and major roads is expected to significantly reduce natural cover. Importantly, these results underscore the greater relevance of our integrated approach, which is applicable to others like it.

## Introduction

More than 50% of the world’s population lives within 50 km of the coast and depends on marine and terrestrial ecosystems for their livelihoods [[1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR1 "IHO (2017) Spatial data infrastructures “The Marine Dimension.” Guid Hydrogr Off C–17:1–45")]. These ecosystems are increasingly recognized as interconnected socio-ecological systems shaped by dynamic interactions between natural processes and human activities [[2](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR2 "Thurstan RH (2022) The potential of historical ecology to aid understanding of human–ocean interactions throughout the Anthropocene. J Fish Biol 101:351–364. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1111/jfb.15000
                  
                ")]. While urbanization has brought important benefits and improvements in quality of life, uncontrolled and excessive growth has generated multiple negative impacts, including environmental pollution, loss of agricultural land, deforestation, and irreversible land-use conversion [[3](#ref-CR3 "Yin H, Kong F, Yang X et al (2018) Exploring zoning scenario impacts upon urban growth simulations using a dynamic spatial model. Cities 81:214–229. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2018.04.010
                  
                "),[4](#ref-CR4 "Liu D, Clarke KC, Chen N (2020) Integrating spatial nonstationarity into SLEUTH for urban growth modeling: a case study in the Wuhan metropolitan area. Comput Environ Urban Syst 84:101545. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compenvurbsys.2020.101545
                  
                "),[5](#ref-CR5 "Chadchan J, Shankar R (2012) An analysis of urban growth trends in the post-economic reforms period in India. Int J Sustain Built Environ 1:36–49. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijsbe.2012.05.001
                  
                "),[6](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR6 "Halmy MWA, Gessler PE, Hicke JA, Salem BB (2015) Land use/land cover change detection and prediction in the north-western coastal desert of Egypt using Markov-CA. Appl Geogr 63:101–112. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apgeog.2015.06.015
                  
                ")]. In the Peruvian context, particularly in coastal regions such as Ica, human settlements and economic activities have transformed the landscape due to population growth, migration, and the expansion of agricultural and industrial activities.

Understanding the dynamics of land-use and land-cover (LULC) change is essential for sustainable land-use planning and proper ecosystem management [[7](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR7 "Guan DJ, Li HF, Inohae T et al (2011) Modeling urban land use change by the integration of cellular automaton and Markov model. Ecol Modell 222:3761–3772. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2011.09.009
                  
                "), [8](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR8 "Jiao L, Dong T, Xu G et al (2021) Geographic micro-process model: understanding global urban expansion from a process-oriented view. Comput Environ Urban Syst 87:101603. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compenvurbsys.2021.101603
                  
                ")]. To this end, various models have been developed to simulate and predict these dynamics, which are key for decision-making [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR9 "Kushwaha K, Singh MM, Singh SK, Patel A (2021) Urban growth modeling using earth observation datasets, cellular Automata-Markov Chain model and urban metrics to measure urban footprints. Remote Sens Appl Soc Environ. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rsase.2021.100479
                  
                ")]. These models can be grouped into three main categories: (i) empirical and statistical models (e.g., Markov chains and regression models), (ii) dynamic models (e.g., cellular automata (CA), agent-based models), and (iii) integrated models [[10](#ref-CR10 "Munshi T, Zuidgeest M, Brussel M, van Maarseveen M (2014) Logistic regression and cellular automata-based modelling of retail, commercial and residential development in the city of Ahmedabad, India. Cities 39:68–86. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2014.02.007
                  
                "),[11](#ref-CR11 "González-Méndez M, Olaya C, Fasolino I et al (2021) Agent-based modeling for urban development planning based on human needs. Conceptual basis and model formulation. Land Use Policy. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.landusepol.2020.105110
                  
                "),[12](#ref-CR12 "Valbuena D, Verburg PH, Bregt AK, Ligtenberg A (2010) An agent-based approach to model land-use change at a regional scale. Landsc Ecol 25:185–199. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s10980-009-9380-6
                  
                "),[13](#ref-CR13 "Hasan ME, Nath B, Sarker AHMR et al (2020) Applying multi-temporal landsat satellite data and markov-cellular automata to predict forest cover change and forest degradation of sundarban reserve forest, Bangladesh. Forests. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/F11091016
                  
                "),[14](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR14 "Hu Z, Lo CP (2007) Modeling urban growth in Atlanta using logistic regression. Comput Environ Urban Syst 31:667–688. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.compenvurbsys.2006.11.001
                  
                ")]. Among these, CA models stand out for their ability to simulate spatio-temporal dynamics by applying transition rules to individual cells based on the conditions of their surroundings [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR3 "Yin H, Kong F, Yang X et al (2018) Exploring zoning scenario impacts upon urban growth simulations using a dynamic spatial model. Cities 81:214–229. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2018.04.010
                  
                "), [15](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR15 "Arsanjani JJ, Helbich M, Kainz W, Boloorani AD (2013) Integration of logistic regression, Markov chain and cellular automata models to simulate urban expansion. Int J Appl Earth Obs Geoinf 21:265–275. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jag.2011.12.014
                  
                ")].

The use of remote sensing technologies has significantly improved the capacity to monitor and analyze land-use changes at large spatial and temporal scales, providing cost-effective and complementary data to field surveys [[16](#ref-CR16 "Carter V (1977) Coastal wetlands: the present and future role of remote sensing"),[17](#ref-CR17 "Jensen JR (1993) An evaluation of the coastwatch change detection protocol in South Carolina. Photogramm Eng Remote Sens 59:1039–1046"),[18](#ref-CR18 "Wang Y (2010) Remote sensing of coastal environments. Taylor & F. Florida"),[19](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR19 "Klemas V (2011) Remote sensing techniques for studying coastal ecosystems: an overview. J Coast Res 27:2–17. 
                  https://doi.org/10.2112/JCOASTRES-D-10-00103.1
                  
                ")]. The combination of satellite imagery with geographic information systems (GIS) has facilitated the development of spatially explicit ecological models (SEM), which are essential for understanding the relationships between landscape dynamics and biodiversity [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR20 "Lizundia-Loiola J, Otón G, Ramo R, Chuvieco E (2020) A spatio-temporal active-fire clustering approach for global burned area mapping at 250 m from MODIS data. Remote Sens Environ 236:111493. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2019.111493
                  
                "), [21](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR21 "Ruben GB, Zhang K, Dong Z, Xia J (2020) Analysis and projection of land-use/land-cover dynamics through scenario-based simulations using the CA-Markov model: a case study in guanting reservoir basin, China. Sustain. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su12093747
                  
                ")]. Despite these advances, studies focused on the coastal region of Ica, Peru, are still scarce. Previous research has documented rapid urban expansion, the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses, and ecosystem degradation due to industrial and mining activities [[22](#ref-CR22 "Diaconu DC, Peptenatu D, Gruia AK et al (2025) The impact of urban expansion on land use in emerging territorial systems: case study Bucharest-Ilfov, Romania. Agric 15:1–15. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/agriculture15040406
                  
                "),[23](#ref-CR23 "Zubair OA, Ji W, Festus O (2019) Urban expansion and the loss of prairie and agricultural lands: a satellite remote-sensing-based analysis at a sub-watershed scale. Sustain. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/su11174673
                  
                "),[24](#ref-CR24 "Bikis A, Engdaw M, Pandey D, Pandey BK (2025) The impact of urbanization on land use land cover change using geographic information system and remote sensing: a case of Mizan Aman City Southwest Ethiopia. Sci Rep 15:1–24. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-025-94189-6
                  
                "),[25](#ref-CR25 "Salmoral G, Viñarta Carbó A, Zegarra E et al (2020) Reconciling irrigation demands for agricultural expansion with environmental sustainability—a preliminary assessment for the Ica Valley, Peru. J Clean Prod. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.123544
                  
                "),[26](#ref-CR26 "Bernal I, Tavera H, Sulla W et al (2018) Geomorphology characterization of Ica basin and its influence on the dynamic response of soils for urban seismic hazards in Ica, Peru. Int J Geophys. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1155/2018/9434251
                  
                "),[27](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR27 "Luis J, Esteban R, César J, Rojas M (2022) Crecimiento poblacional, cambio de uso de suelo y su impacto en los recursos hídricos en la cuenca del rio Ica, Perú. Cienc Lat Rev Científica Multidiscip 6:1485–1502. 
                  https://doi.org/10.37811/cl_rcm.v6i3.2307
                  
                ")]. However, many of these studies have focused on specific sectors or cities, do not include projections using dynamic models, such as Markov chains and cellular automata, or lack a comprehensive regional approach. This highlights the need for studies that quantify historical changes and at the same time project future scenarios to support sustainable land management.

The Ica department (Peru) has undergone significant land-use and land-cover transformations in recent decades, driven by urban expansion, agricultural intensification, and industrial development. However, knowledge of the spatio-temporal patterns and drivers of these changes at a regional scale remains limited, and existing studies do not sufficiently explore future scenarios using integrated dynamic models. This study aims to analyze the dynamics of land-use and land-cover change in Ica between 1990 and 2020 and to project scenarios to 2030 using random forest classification technique was used to perform image classification, while LULC changes were performed using change detection techniques. Specifically, it seeks to (i) map and quantify historical changes, (ii) model transitions and main change probabilities, and (iii) simulate future scenarios to support sustainable land-use planning in the region.

## Materials and methods

### Study area

The study area is located in the department of Ica (Peru), approximately 13° 31′ 59ʺ and 14° 1′ 47ʺ south latitude, and 76° 30′ 7ʺ and 75° 39′ 18" west longitude (Fig. [1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#Fig1)). The study area was divided into four areas of interest (A1 to A4, with 226,303.23 ha), considering the area of influence of National Reserves (NR). A1: continental area of the NR, A2: official buffer zone of Paracas NR, A3 and A4: two intentionally delimited zones of influence with a radius of 10 km each. The city of Ica represents about 36% of the department’s total surface area [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR26 "Bernal I, Tavera H, Sulla W et al (2018) Geomorphology characterization of Ica basin and its influence on the dynamic response of soils for urban seismic hazards in Ica, Peru. Int J Geophys. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1155/2018/9434251
                  
                ")]. According to the 2017 census, the population of Ica was around 850,000 inhabitants [[28](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR28 "INEI (2022) Perú: Proyecciones de población, según departamento, provincia y distrito, 2018–2020. INEI, Lima")], with steady growth driven by immigration linked to employment opportunities in agriculture and tourism [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR25 "Salmoral G, Viñarta Carbó A, Zegarra E et al (2020) Reconciling irrigation demands for agricultural expansion with environmental sustainability—a preliminary assessment for the Ica Valley, Peru. J Clean Prod. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.123544
                  
                ")]. The climate is warm and dry throughout the year, characteristic of a desert region, with annual precipitation not exceeding 10 mm and an average temperature of 22 °C, although maximum temperatures can reach 28 °C and minimums can drop to 15 °C [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR25 "Salmoral G, Viñarta Carbó A, Zegarra E et al (2020) Reconciling irrigation demands for agricultural expansion with environmental sustainability—a preliminary assessment for the Ica Valley, Peru. J Clean Prod. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.123544
                  
                "), [27](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR27 "Luis J, Esteban R, César J, Rojas M (2022) Crecimiento poblacional, cambio de uso de suelo y su impacto en los recursos hídricos en la cuenca del rio Ica, Perú. Cienc Lat Rev Científica Multidiscip 6:1485–1502. 
                  https://doi.org/10.37811/cl_rcm.v6i3.2307
                  
                ")]. The main ecosystems include coastal desert, seasonally dry riparian forest (algarrobal), coastal wetlands, and Andean scrubland [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR25 "Salmoral G, Viñarta Carbó A, Zegarra E et al (2020) Reconciling irrigation demands for agricultural expansion with environmental sustainability—a preliminary assessment for the Ica Valley, Peru. J Clean Prod. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2020.123544
                  
                ")]. The coastal zone is part of an ecologically or biologically significant marine area (EBSA) and hosts an RAMSAR site that overlaps with the Marine Protected Area (MPA) and its buffer zone. The land-cover map identifies the main land uses in coastal and Andean agricultural and urban areas [[29](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR29 "MINAM (2019) Mapa Nacional de Ecosistemas del Perú: Memoria Descriptiva. Dirección General de Ordenamiento Territorial Ambiental, Lima"), [30](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR30 "MINAM (2019) Mapa Nacional de Ecosistemas del Perú—Memoria Descriptiva. Minist del Ambient, pp 1–124")]. The physiography of the region varies from plains and rolling hills to moderately steep mountain slopes [[31](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR31 "MINAGRI (1994) Mapa Fisiográfico del Perú. MINAGRI")].

The area also encompasses terrestrial and marine zones under official protection, such as the Paracas NR (PNR) and the Chincha and Ballestas Islands, which are part of the National Reserve System of Islands, Islets, and Guanera Points. The boundaries of these protected areas safeguard over a hundred important archaeological sites associated with the Paracas culture. The PNR is recognized under the Convention on Biological Diversity (CBD) as an EBSA and, additionally, as a Ramsar Wetland due to its high biodiversity, which includes more than 400 coastal and marine species, including endemic, threatened, and migratory species [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR32 "Valdés-Velásquez A, Diz D, Forsberg K, et al (2023) Mainstreaming biodiversity into local coastal development: key findings for the Paracas Bay Area, Peru"), [33](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR33 "Franke I (2006) La Conservación de las Aves Acuáticas para las Américas. Lima")]. In the Paracas Bay, various activities such as mining, industrial manufacturing, artisanal fishing, aquaculture, and tourism have generated cumulative impacts on marine–coastal ecosystems [[34](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR34 "Bakun A, Weeks SJ (2008) The marine ecosystem off Peru: what are the secrets of its fishery productivity and what might its future hold? Prog Oceanogr 79:290–299. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pocean.2008.10.027
                  
                "), [35](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR35 "Simeoni C, Furlan E, Pham HV et al (2023) Evaluating the combined effect of climate and anthropogenic stressors on marine coastal ecosystems: insights from a systematic review of cumulative impact assessment approaches. Sci Total Environ. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.160687
                  
                ")]. Pressure on the protected area has increased due to inadequate land management. Understanding the complexity of spatial use in Paracas Bay, therefore, requires analyzing not only current spatial dynamics but also the historical, social, and political factors that have shaped these landscapes over time [[32](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR32 "Valdés-Velásquez A, Diz D, Forsberg K, et al (2023) Mainstreaming biodiversity into local coastal development: key findings for the Paracas Bay Area, Peru")].

### Methodological workflow of LULC

Figure [2](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#Fig2) shows the methodological process for assessing LULC dynamics using spatial analysis and cloud computing in a coastal tropical ecosystem in Peru. The first step was the acquisition of remote sensing data for 1990, 2000, 2010 and 2020 from Landsat 5 and 8 satellites to then obtain LULC maps using random forest classification. The second step was to identify predictor variables to predict the LULC to 2030 using automata cellular-based land change modeler.

### Data and preprocessing

The data acquisition consisted of selecting satellite images from Landsat 5 (ID: LANDSAT/LT05/C01/T1) and Landsat 8 (ID: LANDSAT/LC08/C01/T1\_TOA) collections for 1990, 2000, 2010 and 2020. In addition, the Shuttle Radar Topography Mission (SRTM) digital elevation model was used to generate the slope map [[36](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR36 "Farr TG, Rosen PA, Caro E et al (2007) The shuttle radar topography mission. Rev Geophys 45:1–33. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1029/2005RG000183
                  
                ")]. The first step was to select the dates from January 1 to December 31 of each year of analysis. Subsequently, annual mosaics were constructed and selected with a cloud cover percentage of less than 20%. However, it was necessary to perform a correction of clouds and cloud shadows in each mosaic following that proposed by Barboza et al. [[37](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR37 "Barboza E, Turpo EY, Tariq A et al (2024) Spatial distribution of burned areas from 1986 to 2023 using cloud computing: a case study in Amazonas (Peru). Fire. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/fire7110413
                  
                ")], to then calculate the spectral indices according to Table [1](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#Tab1), which served to improve the prediction model of the LULC types [[38](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR38 "Aguilar R, Zurita-Milla R, Izquierdo-Verdiguier E, de By RA (2018) A cloud-based multi-temporal ensemble classifier to map smallholder farming systems. Remote Sens. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/rs10050729
                  
                ")]. The whole process was implemented in the Google Earth Engine (GEE) platform [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR39 "Gorelick N, Hancher M, Dixon M et al (2017) Google Earth Engine: planetary-scale geospatial analysis for everyone. Remote Sens Environ 202:18–27. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2017.06.031
                  
                ")].

### Thematic classification and accuracy assessment of LULC

For LULC classification the selection machine learning Random Forest (RF) classification algorithm [[46](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR46 "Breiman L (2001) Random forests. In: Machine learning. pp 5–32")] was selected due to its advantages over other classification algorithms (i.e., flexibility, ease of use, and combined result of multiple decision trees) and its capabilities to achieve adequate mapping accuracies [[47](#ref-CR47 "Nguyen LH, Joshi DR, Clay DE, Henebry GM (2020) Characterizing land cover/land use from multiple years of Landsat and MODIS time series: a novel approach using land surface phenology modeling and random forest classifier. Remote Sens Environ 238:111017. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2018.12.016
                  
                "),[48](#ref-CR48 "Talukdar S, Eibek KU, Akhter S et al (2021) Modeling fragmentation probability of land-use and land-cover using the bagging, random forest and random subspace in the Teesta River Basin, Bangladesh. Ecol Indic 126:107612. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolind.2021.107612
                  
                "),[49](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR49 "Barboza E, Salazar W, Gálvez-Paucar D, et al (2022) Cover and land use changes in the dry forest of Tumbes (Peru) using Sentinel-2 and Google Earth Engine Data. 2. 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/iecf2022-13095
                  
                ")]. RF is a supervised machine learning method based on a set of decision trees [[50](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR50 "Breiman L (2001) Random forests. Mach Learn 45:5–32. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1010933404324
                  
                ")]. Its fundamental principle is that of “ensemble” or aggregation: it combines multiple decision trees constructed from different subsets of the data and predictor variables, randomly selected at each iteration. Each tree contributes one vote and the final class is decided by majority vote (classification) or average (regression). This approach reduces the risk of overfitting, improves generalization and increases model stability and accuracy. 200 training areas were manually identified using high-resolution imagery such as Google Earth Pro, which were randomly distributed within classes (i) desert, (ii) crops, (iii) urban and (iv) continental water, ensuring a balanced representation of pixel values across land-cover types. These samples were split for input to the RF algorithm, with 70% used for model training and 30% for validation. Accuracy was assessed using the kappa index [[51](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR51 "Mehra N, Swain JB (2024) Suitability of satellite data for urbanization study: a comparative analysis. J Inst Eng Ser A. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s40030-024-00832-2
                  
                "), [52](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR52 "Thanh Noi P, Kappas M (2017) Comparison of random forest, k-nearest neighbor, and support vector machine classifiers for land cover classification using sentinel-2 imagery. Sensors (Basel). 
                  https://doi.org/10.3390/s18010018
                  
                ")].

This allowed an accuracy assessment among qualitative parameters [[53](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR53 "Cohen J (1960) A coefficient of agreement for nominal scales. Educ Psychol Meas 20:37–46"), [54](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR54 "Heydari SS, Mountrakis G (2018) Effect of classifier selection, reference sample size, reference class distribution and scene heterogeneity in per-pixel classification accuracy using 26 Landsat sites. Remote Sens Environ 204:648–658. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2017.09.035
                  
                ")]. The classification process was performed through GEE [[39](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR39 "Gorelick N, Hancher M, Dixon M et al (2017) Google Earth Engine: planetary-scale geospatial analysis for everyone. Remote Sens Environ 202:18–27. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rse.2017.06.031
                  
                ")], an open-access platform and tool that links the potential of big data and cloud processing efficiency. It was used to classify satellite images for later export and subsequently converted to vector format in ArcGIS Pro v. 3.0.0 (ESRI, CA, USA) for spatial analysis and mapping.

The classification evaluation was carried out using standard metrics widely accepted in remote sensing studies, such as user accuracy, producer accuracy, commission error and omission error, complemented by overall accuracy and the Kappa index using 196 validation points proposed by Chuvieco [[55](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR55 "Chuvieco E (2016) Fundamentals of satellite remote sensing an environmental approach, 2nd edn. CRC Press")].

### Land use and land cover to 2030

Markov chains and cellular automata (CA) are widely used in dynamic spatial models to predict future LULC changes and assess the socio-environmental consequences of land transformation [[3](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR3 "Yin H, Kong F, Yang X et al (2018) Exploring zoning scenario impacts upon urban growth simulations using a dynamic spatial model. Cities 81:214–229. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cities.2018.04.010
                  
                "), [7](/articles/10.1186/s12302-025-01181-y#ref-CR7 "Guan DJ, Li HF, Inohae T et al (2011) Modeling urban land use change by the integration of cellular automaton and Markov model. Ecol Modell 222:3761–3772. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolmodel.2011.09.009
                  
                ")]. In these models, GIS and remote sensing data are integrated to analyze and detect changes over time [[56](#ref-CR56 "Raziq A, Xu A, Li Y (2016) Monitoring of land use/land cover changes and urban sprawl in Peshawar City in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa: an application of geo-information techniques using of multi-temporal satellite data. J Remote Sens GIS. 
                  https://doi.org/10.4172/2469-4134.1000174
                  
                "),[57](#ref-CR57 "Barboza E, Turpo EY, Lopez RS et al (2024) Analyzing urban expansion and land use dynamics in Bagua Grande and Chachapoyas using cloud computing and predictive modeling. Earth Syst Environ. 
                  https://doi.org/10.1007/s41748-0

enriched_points.expanded_content: Future land use changes were modeled using CA-Markov techniques, indicating a significant shift towards agricultural expansion, particularly in regions historically dominated by forests and grasslands.
Results were illustrated through detailed spatial maps and graphs, providing clear visual representations of projected land use changes over time, enhancing interpretability for stakeholders.
The model outputs were validated against historical land use data from Landsat imagery, highlighting trends and accuracy in predicting future transitions within the landscape.
